elements.sent-2411-002: Human walking gait emerges from the intricate interplay between neural motor control, reflexes, muscle dynamics, and passive structures.
elements.sent-2411-003: Notably, ankle mechanics play a crucial role, with the ankle plantar flexor muscles contributing up to \SI{78 {\% of the total positive muscle work during push-off \cite{Meinders1998 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, the elastic recoil of the mechanically passive Achilles tendon is responsible for up to \SI{91 {\% of the ankle power output during push-off \cite{Cronin2013 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: A catapult mechanism in the human leg was proposed to generate the high power output at the ankle by gradually storing energy during stance and then quickly releasing the energy during push-off \cite{Hof1983, Lipfert2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-006: A possible catapult release mechanism could include the passive flexion of the knee joint, as the main part of knee flexion was reported to happen passively after leading leg touch-down \cite{Perry1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Building on Perry's observation, we investigate the effects of passive versus active knee flexion initiation on gait events around push-off using a robotic system.
elements.sent-2411-008: By leveraging the precision of robotic measurements, we aim to elucidate the importance of timing of gait events and its impact on momentum and kinetic energy changes in the trailing leg, remaining body, and center of mass.
elements.sent-2411-009: Understanding the biological mechanisms driving natural leg dynamics during push-off is essential for advancing gait rehabilitation \cite{Kapsalyamov2024 , prosthesis \cite{tran2022 , orthosis, and exoskeleton development \cite{molinaro2024 , as well as efficient legged robot design \cite{Ames2014 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The principles of passive dynamics underlying human gait have been extensively investigated with robots \cite{Mcgeer1990, Collins2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, none of the passively walking bipedal robots based on passive dynamics have been studying the human lower-leg catapult function specifically.
elements.sent-2411-012: Instead, they were designed with other goals in mind; the Cornell biped was designed for minimal cost of transport, the MIT learning biped was designed to test motor learning utility on a passive-dynamic mechanical design, and the Delft biped was designed to test skateboard-like ankle joints for lateral stability \cite{Collins2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The EcoWalker-2 robot, a new version of the EcoWalker robot \cite{Kiss2022 , in contrast to the crouched gait of many humanoids is capable of straight-leg walking gait.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Straight-leg walking is more human-like and it improves energy efficiency as the required torque and energy consumption to support the body weight at the knee joints is lower than during bent-knee walking \cite{Winter2009, Kurazume2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: A robot walking with straight legs in midstance can achieve greater ground clearance, enabling it to navigate over larger obstacles and reduce the risk of collisions, while also reducing the necessary range of motion and actuator velocities required to maintain a given walking speed \cite{Griffin2018 .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \sidesubfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.46\textwidth]{figures/lit_views.png}\label{fig:intro_views}} \hfil \sidesubfloat[]{\includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{figures/intro_timing_plot_.png}\label{fig:intro_timing_graph}} \caption{The two main roles of push-off (a) and the expected effect of passive versus active knee flexion initiation (b). The main part of knee flexion, from 5-\SI{40}{deg}, occurs passively after LLTD \cite{Perry1992}. We compare \textcolor{bronze}{\pkf{}} and \textcolor{rosa_d}{\akf{}} to influence energy flow into the \textcolor{rosa_b}{RB} and \textcolor{cyan_d}{TL} by indirectly manipulating SAPF timing. We aim to contribute to the debate on the primary function of ankle push-off in the human swing leg catapult mechanism \cite{Zelik2016}. a: During a step-to-step transition, push-off plays two main roles: it redirects the center of mass velocity ($\Delta \mathbf{v}_\mathrm{CoM}$) from one inverted pendulum arc to the next, and it increases the velocity of the \textcolor{cyan_d}{TL}. The drawing was inspired by Fig 1 in \cite{Adamczyk2009} and Fig 4 in \cite{Lipfert2014}. b: We designed the \textcolor{rosa_d}{\akf{}} experiment with earlier knee flexion onset than \textcolor{bronze}{\pkf{}} experiment to alter the SAPF timing. This results in an earlier SAPF in \textcolor{rosa_d}{\akf{}}, prolonging the positive ankle power ($\mathrm{P}_\mathrm{ank, pos}$) period until LLTD. Before LLTD, the \textcolor{cyan_d}{TL} bears the whole body weight, while the loading leg (LL) is in swing. The filled part under the positive ankle power curve represents the predicted ankle energy mainly accelerating the \textcolor{rosa_b}{RB}. Due to the predicted SAPF delay in \textcolor{bronze}{\pkf{}} experiment, we expect lower ankle energy accelerating the \textcolor{rosa_b}{RB} in \textcolor{bronze}{\pkf{}} than in \textcolor{rosa_d}{\akf{}} experiment. Abbreviations: \textcolor{cyan_d}{TL: Trailing Leg}, LL: Leading Leg, \textcolor{rosa_b}{RB: Remaining Body}, CoM: Center of Mass, vmin: time of minimum vertical velocity of the CoM (start of Step-to-Step transition period), vmax: second vertical velocity peak of the CoM after vmin (end of Step-to-Step transition period), SAPF: Start of Ankle Plantar Flexion, LLTD: Leading Leg Touch-Down, TO: Toe-Off, \textcolor{rosa_d}{\akf{}: Active Knee Flexion Initiation}, \textcolor{bronze}{\pkf{}: Passive Knee Flexion Initiation}.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-016: To achieve efficient walking, the EcoWalker-2 robot draws inspiration from the human lower leg's unique mechanical properties.
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, the human lower leg can be thought of as a catapult system, where elastic energy is stored and rapidly released to facilitate movement.
elements.sent-2411-018: A catapult has three main mechanical components: an elastic element, a block, and a catch.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the human lower leg, it is challenging to identify the specific catapult mechanics and their functional elements due to the complex interplay between the thigh-shank-foot segment chain and muscle-tendon units at the ankle joint.
elements.sent-2411-020: Elastic energy storage and rapid recoil during ankle push-off are facilitated by the Achilles tendon, which is attached to the Soleus (SOL) and Gastrocnemius (GAS) muscles \cite{Fukunaga.2001,Ishikawa.2005,Lichtwark.2006,Lichtwark.2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The GAS muscle's mostly isometric work facilitates energy-efficient loading of the Achilles tendon, while the SOL muscle contributes to ankle coordination through active contraction \cite{Cronin.2013c .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The EcoWalker robot was used to demonstrate the benefits of a SOL spring-tendon, which amplified ankle power and enabled more efficient and faster walking \cite{Kiss2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: In contrast, a GAS spring-tendon was shown to modify the coordination between the ankle and knee joints during ankle push-off \cite{Kiss2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The block, serving as the supporting structure of the catapult, was determined to be a combination of the foot and the ground \cite{Renjewski2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The catch, a crucial component that locks and releases the human leg catapult, remains unidentified.
elements.sent-2411-026: During the release of the catapult, the knee and the hip start to flex, and the ankle starts to plantarflex.
elements.sent-2411-027: A possible release mechanism can be derives from Perry's work \cite{Perry1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The main part of knee flexion, from \SI{5 {\degree to \SI{40 {\degree , was reported to happen passively after leading leg touch-down, as weight transfer to the leading leg frees the trailing leg's knee joint to flex passively \cite{Perry1992 (p.. 104f).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this work and for the first time, we test Perry's passive knee flexion process on a bipedal robot, as part of the lock-and-release mechanism of the human leg catapult.
elements.sent-2411-030: We test if passive knee flexion resulting from leg dynamics can trigger the human leg catapult release on a bipedal robot.
elements.sent-2411-031: We compare the same robotic system, the EcoWalker-2 robot \cite{Kiss2022 , with and without active knee flexion initiation: active knee flexion initiation (\akf{ ) versus passive knee flexion initiation (\pkf{ ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In the \pkf{ experiment, the trailing leg's knee motor does not apply torque in the EcoWalker-2 robot \cite{Kiss2022 , the knee joint can move freely, from midstance until the next touch-down of the trailing leg.}}

elements.sent-2411-039: At the start of ankle plantar flexion the ankle power becomes positive, indicating the start of ankle unloading.

elements.sent-2411-048: By manipulating the timing of the start of ankle plantar flexion, we intend to influence the ratio of energy flow into the remaining body and the trailing leg.
elements.sent-2411-049: Accurately studying the effects of such a gait event timing change can be challenging due to the inherent variability in human gait data, where the average standard deviation of gait events can be as high as \SI{1.5 {\% of the gait cycle \cite{VanCriekinge2023 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-051: The almost tenfold better precision of robotic measurements allowed us to investigate the crucial role of timing of gait events and its influence on momentum and kinetic energy changes in the trailing leg, remaining body, and center of mass.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our investigation into the effects of timing of gait events has wide-ranging implications for understanding the main function of push-off, a topic of ongoing debate in the field.
elements.sent-2411-053: Some researchers (\ls) argue that push-off energy primarily accelerates the swing leg, while others (\rd) propose that it aids in redirecting the center of mass velocity to reduce collision losses \cite{Zelik2016 (\Cref{fig:intro_views ).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, despite the extensive discussion on the function of push-off, the influence of gait event timing on its function has remained largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-055: By examining the factors that influence the push-off energy flow, such as the timing of ankle plantar flexion, we expect to gain a deeper understanding of the function of push-off in gait and contribute to resolving the long-standing debate.

elements.sent-2411-117: The first goal of this study was to test Perry's \cite{Perry1992 passive knee flexion process on a bipedal robot, as part of the lock-and-release mechanism of the human swing leg catapult.}
elements.sent-2411-118: We showed that the EcoWalker-2 robot is able to walk with sufficient toe clearance both with passive knee flexion initiation (\pkf{ ) and with active knee flexion initiation (\akf{ ).}}
elements.sent-2411-119: The second aim of the study was to influence the ratio of energy flow into the remaining body and the trailing leg by indirectly manipulating the timing of the start of the ankle plantar flexion.
elements.sent-2411-120: We showed that ankle plantar flexion starts significantly later in the \pkf{ experiment than in the \akf{ experiment.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: This delay had a pronounced effect on the momentum changes during the step-to-step transition.
elements.sent-2411-122: Notably, the magnitude increase in the trailing leg momentum vector during the step-to-step transition was significantly larger (\SI{77 {\% ), the magnitude decrease in the remaining body momentum vector during the step-to-step transition was significantly larger (\SI{19 {\% ), and the magnitude increase in the center of mass momentum vector during the step-to-step transition was significantly larger (\SI{188 {\% ) in the \pkf{ experiment than in the \akf{ experiment.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: As expected, starting the knee flexion earlier in the \akf{ experiment enables an earlier onset of ankle plantar flexion (\Cref{fig:timing_graph ).}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The timing delay of the start of ankle plantar flexion gives further evidence of Perry's hypothesis that knee flexion is needed for ankle unloading \cite{Perry1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-125: Surprisingly, leading leg touch-down did not happen exactly at \SI{50 {\% of the gait cycle (GC) in either \pkf{ or \akf{ experiment by neither legs (\Cref{fig:timing_graph , see Supplementary Information S2).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: The 1-\SI{3 {\%GC earlier or later leading leg touch-down could be a result of the slight asymmetry between the right and left sides of the robot.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Even though the slack positions of the GAS and SOL spring-tendons were set symmetrically, there was always a little difference between the left and right, hip and knee joint actuators which causes a minor asymmetry in the robot gait.
elements.sent-2411-128: Asymmetry in human gait is also a common phenomenon, with strength imbalances present even in young adults.
elements.sent-2411-129: Research has shown that young adults typically exhibit strength asymmetries ranging from \SI{5 {\% to \SI{15 {\% \cite{Perry2007, Lanshammar2011 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: In contrast, older adults tend to develop more pronounced asymmetries, typically in the range of \SI{15 {\% to \SI{20 {\% \cite{Skelton2002, Perry2007 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The time period between the start of ankle plantar flexion and leading leg touch-down was expected to be shorter in \pkf{ experiment than in \akf{ experiment.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: However, the delay in ankle plantar flexion onset was more pronounced than expected in the \pkf{ experiment.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Specifically, ankle plantar flexion occurred \SI{2 {\%GC before leading leg touch-down in the \akf{ experiment, similar to human data \cite{VanderZee2022 , whereas it occurred \SI{2 {\%GC after leading leg touch-down in the \pkf{ experiment.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The start of ankle plantar flexion means the start of ankle energy unloading where the ankle power curve becomes positive.
elements.sent-2411-135: Both in the \pkf{ and \akf{ experiments, the event flow of the step-to-step transition can only start after the start of knee flexion.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: As the start of knee flexion is delayed in the \pkf{ experiment, all gait events of the trailing leg are delayed.}
elements.sent-2411-137: In human experimental gait data, the start of knee flexion was reported to happen before the start of ankle plantar flexion and before leading leg touch-down \cite{Perry1992, Lipfert2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-138: Knee flexion appears to play a crucial role in initiating the step-to-step transition, with its timing having a cascading effect on subsequent gait events.
elements.sent-2411-139: With the \pkf{ and \akf{ experiments, we characterized two distinct disengagement processes on the same robotic system.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: In the \akf{ experiment, as ankle unloading started before leading leg touch-down, hip and knee flexion pulled the ankle joint upwards, making it possible for the ankle joint to start plantar flexing.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Part of the unloading happened against the whole body weight before leading leg touch-down, a mechanism known as preemptive push-off, which has been well documented in human experiments \cite{Fukunaga2001, ishikawa2005muscle, Lichtwark.2007, Cronin2013, Perry1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-142: In humans, preemptive push-off is initiated by gastrocnemius (GAS) and soleus (SOL) muscle activations, while in the robot, it is initiated by active hip and active knee flexion.
elements.sent-2411-143: After leading leg touch-down, only elastic recoil happens in humans as the observed GAS and SOL muscle activations diminish \cite{Fukunaga2001, ishikawa2005muscle, Lichtwark.2007, Cronin2013, Perry1992 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: After leading leg touch-down, the robot's hip and knee joints remain active, facilitating the rest of ankle unloading against the trailing leg through a dual mechanism of elastic recoil and active knee and hip flexion.
elements.sent-2411-145: In literature, this preemptive push-off is explained as a method to restore heel-strike collision losses \cite{McGeer.1990, Donelan2002_mechWork, Donelan2002_simPosNeg, Kuo2002, Collins2005, Dean2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-146: By using active knee and hip flexion with a passive ankle joint, we could reach a similar preemptive push-off.
elements.sent-2411-147: Notably, increasing hip work to facilitate leg swing has been shown to reduce ankle muscle activations during push-off \cite{Lenzi2013 , which can be beneficial for reducing peak plantar pressures and treating conditions such as neuropathic plantar ulcers \cite{Mueller1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Conversely, studies have found that increasing ankle push-off work can decrease hip work \cite{Lewis2008, Caputo2014, Koller2015 , highlighting the trade-offs between hip and ankle contributions during gait.}
elements.sent-2411-149: In the \pkf{ experiment, ankle unloading started only after leading leg touch-down, because hip flexion without active knee flexion initiation did not result in an upward pull at the ankle joint.}
elements.sent-2411-150: As the ankle joint is passive, only elastic recoil from the GAS and SOL spring-tendons actuated the ankle plantar flexion.
elements.sent-2411-151: Unloading happened mainly against the trailing leg, as leading leg touch-down already happened.
elements.sent-2411-152: Before leading leg touch-down, the trailing leg bears the whole body weight as the leading leg is in swing.
elements.sent-2411-153: We hypothesized that the ankle energy being unloaded before leading leg touch-down mainly accelerates the remaining body.
elements.sent-2411-154: As the leading leg takes up increasingly more body weight after leading leg touch-down, the trailing leg becomes increasingly free to go into swing.
elements.sent-2411-155: We expected that the proportion of ankle energy unloaded after leading leg touch-down would determine the distribution of energy between the trailing leg and the remaining body.
elements.sent-2411-156: A greater unloading of ankle energy after leading leg touch-down would result in a larger transfer of energy to the trailing leg, accelerating it into swing, while a smaller amount of energy would accelerate the remaining body.
elements.sent-2411-157: In the \akf{ experiment, a small part of the ankle energy was already unloaded before leading leg touch-down but no part of the ankle energy was unloaded before leading leg touch-down in the \pkf{ experiment (see Supplementary Figure S9).}}

elements.sent-2411-185: While comparing robots with varying morphologies and numbers of legs has its limitations, it still offers valuable insights into how design of mechanics, actuators, and control influence energetic performance.
elements.sent-2411-186: Notably, the EcoWalker-2 robot's best relative net cost of transport of \SI{38 {\% ) is comparable to that of BiartHopper's \cite{ruppert_series_2019 , which achieved a relative net cost of transport of \SI{45 {\% .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: However, the latter robot features only a single hip actuator, whereas EcoWalker-2 robot utilizes two knee and two hip actuators for planar walking.
elements.sent-2411-188: With its design inspired by passive dynamic walkers, the Cornell biped \cite{Collins2005 achieved a relative cost of transport between \SI{7 {\% and \SI{26 {\% of a natural runner of similar weight.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The calculation was based on the provided\cite{Collins2005 cost of transport range from 0.05 (mechanical) to 0.2 (total, electrical) at \SI{13 {kg body weight.}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Therefore, EcoWalker-2 robot approaches the efficiency of passive-dynamic walkers, while remaining fully controllable and versatile.
elements.sent-2411-191: We identify three causes for the EcoWalker-2 robot's outstanding energetic efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-192: First, its brushless motors, low gear ratio, and efficiently built actuators contribute to the robot's low cost of transport \cite{Grimminger2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Second, the EcoWalker-2 robot walks with straight legs in mid-stance, i.e., when ground reaction forces are the highest.
elements.sent-2411-194: Without an acting moment arm, the robot's knee actuator is effectively unloaded around midstance.
elements.sent-2411-195: Third, the EcoWalker-2 robot's leg design and control allows harnessing the elastic energy in the spring-loaded ankles.
elements.sent-2411-196: The robot's catapult mechanism then converts the stored energy into combined swing leg motion and forward momentum.
elements.sent-2411-197: So far, the EcoWalker-2 robot is freely walking in the sagittal plane, but it is constrained against lateral motions and pitch rotation.
elements.sent-2411-198: Once a 3D-walking robot, we expect EcoWalker-2 robot's cost of transport to increase due to the required, additional actuation.
elements.sent-2411-199: Our study has limitations as the experiments were done on a bipedal robot which can only walk in sagittal plane and its upper body is stabilized by a 4-bar mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-200: The restrictions of the robot's design and experimental setup could have potentially over emphasized the results as the upper body stabilization and 3D stabilization of the robot were not factored in.
elements.sent-2411-201: In our opinion, concentrating on the legs in the robot's design and on a single plane made it possible to evaluate the sagittal plane effects more clearly with less confounding factors.
elements.sent-2411-202: Human gait data is highly variable, especially if it is about patient population \cite{VanCriekinge2023 , whereas gait events in robots are more reliable, SD < \SI{0.4 {\%GC (see Supplementary Table S2, and Supplementary Table S6).}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: We found that the time sequence of gait events around the step-to-step transition (leading leg touch-down, start of ankle plantarflexion, start of knee and hip flexion) from human gait data is not defined clearly in literature \cite{Perry1992}\cite{Whittle2007}\cite{Richie2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-204: A robotic system makes the gait event identification more reliable, which in turn makes gait analysis, based on gait events, and analysis of event flows more accurate.
elements.sent-2411-205: On a robotic system, we can investigate the push-off event flow, momentum and energy flow more reliably than with human measurements.
elements.sent-2411-206: However the transfer of the results to human gait remains to be explored in future studies.
elements.sent-2411-207: Changing the way of knee flexion initiation changes the timing of the gait events, remaining body, trailing leg and CoM velocities, momentums, and kinetic energies during the step-to-step transition.
elements.sent-2411-208: Although the method of knee flexion initiation has a significant impact during the step-to-step transition, its effect diminishes thereafter and remains negligible for the first \SI{\sim 20 {\% of the gait cycle.}}
elements.sent-2411-209: Moreover, in terms of walking speed and cost of transport, the way of knee flexion initiation does not have a large overall effect on the whole gait cycle which also shows the robustness of human-like walking gaits.
elements.sent-2411-210: Changes in the gait during the step-to-step transition change the interplay between the disengagement process of the trailing leg and the collision process of the leading leg with the ground.
elements.sent-2411-211: By manipulating the way of knee flexion initiation, we showed that the timing of the gait events around push-off, the distribution of kinetic energy, showing in velocity and momentums of the trailing leg and remaining body, the redirection of the CoM velocity vector and the CoM kinetic energy can be influenced.
elements.sent-2411-212: Knee flexion initiation has been identified as a critical factor in ankle unloading and the coordination of gait events at the end of stance, providing insights into the biomechanical mechanisms underlying the step-to-step transition in human walking.
elements.sent-2411-213: These findings have implications for the development of assistive technologies, including orthotics and prosthetics, and may inform gait rehabilitation practices.
elements.sent-2411-214: By optimizing knee flexion timing in these devices and protocols, clinicians and engineers may be able to restore more natural leg dynamics and improve walking efficiency in individuals with gait impairments.

elements.sent-2411-282: We analyzed the step-to-step transition to see the effects of both the push-off of the trailing leg and the touch-down of the leading leg.
elements.sent-2411-283: Step-to-step transition was defined from minimum to maximum vertical center of mass (CoM) velocity \cite{Adamczyk2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-284: In the velocity plots, known as 'CoM hodographs' \cite{Adamczyk2009 , from our experiments (see Supplementary Figure S1), we consistently observe two similar height vertical maximum peaks following the minimum CoM vertical velocity.}

elements.sent-2411-286: The following gait events were identified during post-processing: Start of Ankle Plantar Flexion (SAPF), Toe-Off (TO), Leading Leg Touch-Down (LLTD), Start of Hip Flexion (SHF), and Start of Knee Flexion (SKF) (\Cref{fig:joint_angles ).}
elements.sent-2411-287: Start of Ankle Plantar Flexion (SAPF) occurs when the trailing leg's ankle starts to plantarflex after steadily dorsiflexing during stance.
elements.sent-2411-288: We defined the moment of SAPF when the trailing leg's ankle plantar flexion angle reached its maximum.
elements.sent-2411-289: Toe-Off (TO) of the trailing leg occurs when the trailing leg's foot leaves the ground.
elements.sent-2411-290: After TO, the ankle angle is approximately constant in swing (\Cref{fig:joint_angles ); at the time of TO, the ankle dorsiflexion angle reaches its minimum and then stops to change.}
elements.sent-2411-291: We calculated the gradient \cite{NumPy of the ankle angle, and defined the moment of TO when the smallest ankle dorsiflexion angle occurred within a \SI{40 {ms time window after the largest negative peak in the ankle angle gradient.}}}
elements.sent-2411-292: Leading Leg Touch-Down (LLTD) is identified when the foot of the leading leg makes contact with the ground.
elements.sent-2411-293: Since the ankle angle remains relatively constant during the swing phase, LLTD can be detected by monitoring changes in the leading leg ankle angle.
elements.sent-2411-294: We calculated the gradient of the leading leg ankle angle \cite{NumPy , and defined LLTD as the last instance before TO when the absolute value of the instantaneous ankle angle gradient exceeded \SI{1 {rad/s , indicating the onset of plantarflexion or dorsiflexion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-295: Start of Hip Flexion (SHF) occurs when the hip starts flexing after steadily extending during stance.
elements.sent-2411-296: We calculated the gradient \cite{NumPy of the hip angle, and defined the moment of SHF when the value of the instantaneous hip angle gradient was more than \SI{1 {rad/s .}}}
elements.sent-2411-297: The time period of interest for the search started from the moment of peak hip extension angle and lasted until the end of the gait cycle.
elements.sent-2411-298: Start of Knee Flexion (SKF) occurs when the knee starts to flex during stance phase.
elements.sent-2411-299: We calculated the gradient \cite{NumPy of the knee angle, and defined the moment of SKF when the value of the instantaneous knee angle gradient was more than \SI{1 {rad/s .}}}
elements.sent-2411-300: The time period of interest for the search started from \SI{0.2 {s before SHF and lasted until \SI{0.1 {s after SHF.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-301: We considered the robot as a system of segments, divided into two feet, two shanks (lower leg), two thighs (upper leg), and a trunk (\Cref{fig:robot_design b).}
elements.sent-2411-302: We calculated the velocities at each segment's center of mass using the joint position angles from the encoders at the ankle, knee, and hip joints, at the four-bar mechanism's lower rod, and the potentiometer values that measured the horizontal position of the four-bar slider connector.
elements.sent-2411-303: Using the segments' center of mass velocity vectors ($\mathbf{v _\mathrm{i}$), and the segments' masses ($m_\mathrm{i}$), we calculated the instantaneous linear momentum vectors of each segment ($\mathbf{p _\mathrm{i}$): \begin{equation}\mathbf{p}_\mathrm{i} = m \cdot \mathbf{v}_\mathrm{i}, \label{eq:p_i}\end{equation} where $\mathrm{i}$ indicates one of the body segments: foot, shank, or thigh of the trailing or leading leg, or the trunk.}}
elements.sent-2411-304: From the momentum vectors of the segments, we calculated the following measures: momentum and velocity vectors, and linear kinetic energies of the trailing leg, remaining body, and the CoM (see Supplementary Information S1).
elements.sent-2411-305: The active joint power of the knee and hip joints could be calculated separately for each joint, as each motor was connected to a separate motor driver board and current sensor: \begin{equation}P_\mathrm{joint, side} = I_\mathrm{cal, joint, side} \cdot U_\mathrm{supply}, \label{eq:power_joint}\end{equation} where $P_\mathrm{joint, side}$ is the active knee/hip joint power on the trailing/leading leg side of the robot, $I_\mathrm{cal, joint, side}$ is the measured net current, at the motor driver board of the knee/hip joint on the trailing/leading leg side of the robot, and $U_\mathrm{supply}$ is the supply voltage of the robot, \SI{24 {V .}}
elements.sent-2411-306: For one gait cycle, the net positive energy consumption of the two knee and two hip motors was calculated as the average of the sum of the knee and hip motor powers, after subtracting the idle power consumption of the motor drivers, and removing the negative power values.
elements.sent-2411-307: The robot's net positive cost of transport ($\mathrm{COT}$) was calculated as $\mathrm{COT =\mathrm{E _\mathrm{en /(mgv)$, with $\mathrm{E _\mathrm{en}$ as the net positive energy consumption of the knee and hip motors \cite{Seok.2013,Sprowitz.2013 , $m$ as the robot's mass, $g$ as the gravitational acceleration (\SI{9.81 {m/s^2 ), and $v$ as the average horizontal robot speed over \num{120 gait cycles.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-308: Comparing the EcoWalker-2 robot's net positive cost of transport ($\coten$) in a fair manner to that of other legged hoppers, bipedal robots, and quadrupedal robots of different body mass can be achieved by calculating the robot's relative cost of transport ($\cotre$) \cite{birdbot_2022 .}
